transcript.speaker: Priya
transcript.text: Good evening everyone. Sprint 3 retro — let's keep it honest and constructive. We have about an hour. I'll start with what went well, then we do what didn't, then we agree on actions for Sprint 4. Everyone ready?

transcript.speaker: Rahul
transcript.text: Ready. And I'll say upfront — I think this sprint had a lot to feel good about despite the environment pain.

transcript.speaker: Priya
transcript.text: Agreed. So — what went well. I'll start with the obvious one. Auth module delivered on time. OIDC integration, token refresh, session management — all done, all signed off by client on Wednesday. That was the primary sprint goal and we hit it. Rahul, from your perspective what went well technically?

transcript.speaker: Rahul
transcript.text: Test coverage. We targeted 85% and Neha's team delivered 91%. That's not just a number — the tests actually caught 3 regressions during development that would have been found in QA otherwise. So the coverage was meaningful, not just metric-padding.

transcript.speaker: Neha
transcript.text: I want to add to that. The team was really cooperative this sprint when I raised issues. In previous sprints I'd raise a bug and wait 2 days for it to be fixed. This sprint, critical bugs were being patched same day most of the time. That made a big difference to QA velocity.

transcript.speaker: Suresh
transcript.text: The daily code review process we introduced at the start of Sprint 3 helped a lot. We caught design issues early before they became bigger problems. Ananya, I remember you spotted the token storage anti-pattern in my code on Day 3 — that could have been a security issue if it reached prod.

transcript.speaker: Ananya
transcript.text: Yes, and you caught the redundant re-render in my auth state component. These cross-reviews took maybe 20 minutes a day but saved hours of rework. That should continue in Sprint 4.

transcript.speaker: Rajan
transcript.text: Good. These are genuine improvements. Now — what didn't go well. I want honest answers, not diplomatic ones.

transcript.speaker: Neha
transcript.text: Staging environment parity. This is the second sprint in a row I'm saying this in a retro. We had 3 separate incidents where staging config didn't match what prod expected. That caused test rework, false failures, and one occasion where I marked a test as failed when the code was actually fine — it was just a staging config difference. Each incident cost us half a day minimum.

transcript.speaker: Vikram
transcript.text: And the root cause is the same every time. Central infra team makes changes to prod configuration without notifying our team. I'm not in their change management loop. By the time I find out — usually when Neha hits a failure — the damage is already done. I've raised this 3 times in infra sync meetings. It has not been actioned.

transcript.speaker: Priya
transcript.text: That's a real process gap. What's the ask, Vikram? What do you actually need to prevent this?

transcript.speaker: Vikram
transcript.text: Two things. One — I need to be cc'd on all prod infrastructure change notifications. A simple email forward. Two — ideally, we run a daily automated check that compares key config values between staging and prod and alerts if they drift. That way I catch it before Neha does.

transcript.speaker: Rahul
transcript.text: The daily config drift check is something I've been thinking about too. We could build a lightweight script — it's probably 2-3 hours of work.

transcript.speaker: Rajan
transcript.text: That's worth doing. What else didn't go well?

transcript.speaker: Ananya
transcript.text: Client feedback loop was slow. There were 3 moments this sprint where we made UI decisions and when client finally reviewed them 5-6 days later, they asked for changes. If we had a 2-day feedback cycle instead of 5-6 days, we'd have avoided those rework loops.

transcript.speaker: Priya
transcript.text: That's fair. The client's point of contact was often out of office or unavailable. I should have flagged that earlier and escalated to get a backup reviewer assigned. That's on me.

transcript.speaker: Suresh
transcript.text: I want to add something. Sprint 3 was the first sprint where the sprint backlog didn't change after Day 3. In Sprint 2, scope kept changing mid-sprint and we ended up doing work that wasn't in the original plan. This sprint Priya protected the scope really well. That made a difference.

transcript.speaker: Priya
transcript.text: Thank you Suresh. Okay — action items for Sprint 4. I'm capturing three main ones. First — Vikram, I'll escalate to Rajan sir for the infra cc change and you build the daily config drift check script before Sprint 4 Day 1. Second — the client feedback turnaround — I'll set up a formal review cadence with the client, 48-hour response SLA, and if the primary contact is unavailable I escalate to their PM. Third — daily code reviews continue, we make it official in the sprint process.

transcript.speaker: Rahul
transcript.text: I'd add a fourth. For Sprint 4 — infra dependencies should be identified and tickets raised before the sprint starts, not during. We burned time in Sprint 3 because we raised infra requests on Day 2 of the sprint and then waited. For Sprint 4 let's have Vikram in the pre-sprint planning session.

transcript.speaker: Vikram
transcript.text: 100%. I've been saying this. Please include me from the start.

transcript.speaker: Rajan
transcript.text: Agreed. Vikram is in Sprint 4 planning, no question. Priya, make sure that happens.

transcript.speaker: Priya
transcript.text: Done. Planning session is Tuesday — I'll send you an invite tonight Vikram.

transcript.speaker: Neha
transcript.text: One final thing from me — I'd like a dedicated smoke test environment that we run every morning before standups. Just 5-10 basic checks that tell us the environment is healthy. Right now we find environment issues when I run my first real test case, which wastes my time.

transcript.speaker: Rahul
transcript.text: Smoke tests make sense. Vikram, can you set that up? Basic health check suite, runs at 8:30am daily?

transcript.speaker: Vikram
transcript.text: Yes. I'll have it ready before Sprint 4 Day 1.

transcript.speaker: Rajan
transcript.text: Good retro everyone. Real issues, real solutions. I want to see the action items tracked in JIRA before Monday. Not lost in this call recording. Priya, that's on you.

transcript.speaker: Priya
transcript.text: I'll create the JIRA tickets tonight. Have a good weekend everyone. Sprint 4 — let's start it right.
